reason:
Both answers correctly identify the core legal landscape: CFAA is largely defused post-hiQ/Van Buren for logged-out scraping, breach-of-contract/ToS is the real threat (hiQ ultimately lost on contract), state privacy/data-broker laws (CCPA/CPRA) are a growing exposure, and litigation cost/C&D practicality matters even though the data is public. Neither makes a factual error that would mislead.

The differentiator is Answer B's inclusion of Meta v. Bright Data (2024), where logged-out scraping specifically beat a contract claim - directly on-point precedent that validates the practical recommendation to scrape logged-out only. B also names the CA Delete Act's DROP deletion mechanism, showing slightly more current regulatory detail. Answer A compensates with cited sources for verifiability. Both are well organized and concise with clear practical postures.

It is close, but B's added precedent makes its actionable guidance slightly more legally grounded and useful.</parameter>
</invoke>
